title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot discusses a smuggling operation with Inspector Waddington and a Bell Inspector, dividing the problem into drug distribution, methods of entry into the country, and the identity of the mastermind. The Bell Inspector notes that while drug distribution channels include nightclubs, pharmacies, and various service providers, the authorities are struggling to track the increasing rate of drug entry. Smuggling of precious stones from South Africa, Australia, and the Far East is also a concern.

Poirot theorizes that a flaw in the shipping process allows for the transport of high-value, small-sized items like heroin, cocaine, or gems. He suggests that the smugglers use unwitting carriers, specifically students, to avoid suspicion. Poirot explains a specific scheme involving high-quality, durable backpacks sold on the market. These bags feature a slight difference in stitching at the bottom and a thick, removable lining used to hide contraband. A group, potentially led by a student or someone posing as one, uses these bags to smuggle goods across the English Channel. The student replaces the tampered bag or lining upon returning to Britain to avoid customs detection.

Poirot links this scheme to Walnut Street, noting that the backpacks there were suspiciously cheap. He deduces that a backpack was cut into pieces and thrown into a boiler room garbage bin because a smuggler, fearing police surveillance following an unrelated visit to a dorm supervisor, felt compelled to destroy the evidence. Poirot believes the act of cutting the bag was a desperate attempt to prevent the bag from being traced back to illegal substances.
